narrative_coherence.notes: 3.4 (tokens, training, inference) establishes the foundational mechanics: text-to-token pipeline, next-token prediction, prefill/decode phases, context window. 3.5 (parameters) deepens one concept introduced briefly in 3.4 — 'parameters (also called weights)' — expanding it to scale, scaling laws, quantisation, and model-selection trade-offs. 3.6 (temperature/sampling) picks up the probabilistic output thread planted in 3.4 ('the degree of randomness is controlled by temperature — covered in topic 3.6') and resolves it fully with logits, probability distributions, and sampling strategies. Each topic opens with an explicit back-reference to the prior topic(s) that supplies its prerequisite concepts, confirming intentional chaining. The arc is: what the model sees → what the model learned → how the model picks its next word.

scope_coherence.notes: The module title 'How LLMs Work' is fully covered by the three topics: tokens/training/inference (3.4), parameters/scale (3.5), temperature/sampling (3.6). The module narrative seed also lists 'AI in India today' and 'jagged frontier' but these are session-level narrative framing, not artifact topics within this three-topic module, and their absence from the topic artifacts is correct. The lab activity (Capability Probe / Journal Entry #2) is supported by hands-on exercises in all three topics — each exercise connects to the lab task from a different angle (probabilistic output, parameter-size comparison, temperature tuning).

findings.note: 3.5 reading closes with 'One note on temperature: this is another model setting that affects output behaviour and will be covered in topic 3.6.' This forward-pointer is appropriate and correctly previews 3.6 without teaching the concept prematurely. Not a violation.

'logit' is introduced in 3.6 corpus §5.1 and reading without prior appearance in 3.4 or 3.5. However, it is defined inline on first use ('a logit is just a score — a large positive logit means this token fits well here') so it is not a cold term. Not a violation.

Because the reference is explanatory rather than load-bearing, this is a soft observation, not a prerequisite violation.

jaccard_pairs.note: Overlap is intentional: 3.5 deepens a concept introduced briefly in 3.4. Below 0.6 threshold.
Shared terms are prerequisite context for 3.6, not duplicated teaching. Below 0.6 threshold.
3.6 references parameters only as background; its core concepts are orthogonal. Well below 0.6 threshold.

lab_activity_alignment.notes: Module lab seed: Capability Probe (5 task types: creative, factual, logical, ethical, coding) + Journal Entry #2. 3.4 §9 exercises probabilistic output via repeated prompts — maps to the 'factual' and 'creative' task types and Journal Entry #2. 3.5 §9 exercises parameter-size comparison via two model tiers — maps to the 'logical/coding' task type. 3.6 §9 exercises temperature tuning on factual and creative prompts — directly maps to the Capability Probe's accuracy-vs-creativity dimension and Journal Entry #2. All three topics provide hands-on artifacts that collectively support the lab activity.

audience_level_consistency.notes: All three topics target an absolute-beginner audience and maintain consistent register. Every technical term introduced in each topic is defined on first use with a plain-language analogy before being used in context. 3.5 and 3.6 correctly use back-references ('from topic 3.4', 'as you learned in topic 3.4') rather than redefining concepts cold. No topic shows a register or depth spike relative to its neighbours — all three use the same pattern of analogy-first definition followed by structured tables and worked examples.

verdict: PASS. All five checks (plus audience-level) pass across topics 3.4, 3.5, and 3.6. The module delivers a coherent, progressive arc from token mechanics through parameter scale to sampling behaviour. Terminology is consistent and forward/back references are correctly placed. Two soft observations logged (3.5 forward-pointer to temperature; 3.6 soft reference to 3.5 parameters without formal prerequisite listing) — neither requires remediation.
